title: An extension of the theory of planned behavior to predict pedestrians' violating crossing behavior using structural equation modeling
authors: Hongmei Zhou; Stephanie Ballon Romero; Xiao Qin
research_problem: Examine pedestrians' self-reported violating crossing behavior intentions by applying and extending the Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB), testing instrumental attitude, subjective norm, perceived behavioral control, and extended factors including descriptive norm, perceived risk, and conformity tendency
method_summary: Questionnaire-based survey conducted in Dalian, China with 260 respondents. A scenario about crossing against pedestrian traffic lights was presented. Data reliability was assessed using Cronbach's alpha and confirmatory factor analysis. Structural equation modeling (SEM) was used to test four models: basic TPB, TPB with descriptive norm, TPB with perceived risk, and TPB with conformity tendency. Goodness of fit was evaluated using CMIN/df, RMSEA, and CFI indices.
method_family: Structural Equation Modeling (SEM), Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB) extension
tasks: Predict pedestrians' violating road-crossing behavior intentions
datasets: Survey data from 260 respondents in Dalian, China
metrics: Cronbach's alpha (reliability); Confirmatory Factor Analysis (validity); CMIN/df ratio; RMSEA; PCLOSE; CFI
claim_text: Instrumental attitude was a significant predictor of pedestrian violating crossing behavior intention across all four models tested
limitations: Self-reported behavior may introduce bias, especially for negative behaviors | Relatively small number of items in questionnaire to maintain internal reliability may affect results | Study conducted only in Dalian, China; findings may not generalize to other cultural contexts where pedestrian crossing behavior differs
notes: Model 4 (with instrumental attitude, descriptive norm, and conformity tendency) provided the best fit with CFI of 0.996; Sample: 57.3% female, 42.7% male; 49.2% aged 18-24, 33.1% aged 25-39; 93.1% of respondents disapproved of the violating behavior (family mean=2.93, friends mean=3.20 on 7-point scale); High perceived risk of injury (mean=5.77) and danger to life (mean=5.83) did not translate to significant behavioral prediction

title: An extension of the theory of planned behavior to predict pedestrians' violating crossing behavior using structural equation modeling
authors: Hongmei Zhou; Stephanie Ballon Romero; Xiao Qin
research_problem: Examine pedestrians' self-reported violating crossing behavior intentions by applying and extending the Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB), testing instrumental attitude, subjective norm, perceived behavioral control, and extended factors including descriptive norm, perceived risk, and conformity tendency
method_summary: Questionnaire-based survey conducted in Dalian, China with 260 respondents. A scenario about crossing against pedestrian traffic lights was presented. Data reliability was assessed using Cronbach's alpha and confirmatory factor analysis. Structural equation modeling (SEM) was used to test four models: basic TPB, TPB with descriptive norm, TPB with perceived risk, and TPB with conformity tendency. Goodness of fit was evaluated using CMIN/df, RMSEA, and CFI indices.
method_family: Structural Equation Modeling (SEM), Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB) extension
tasks: Predict pedestrians' violating road-crossing behavior intentions
datasets: Survey data from 260 respondents in Dalian, China
metrics: Cronbach's alpha (reliability); Confirmatory Factor Analysis (validity); CMIN/df ratio; RMSEA; PCLOSE; CFI
claim_text: Descriptive norm was a stronger predictor than subjective norm, and when descriptive norm was added to the model, subjective norm became non-significant
limitations: Self-reported behavior may introduce bias, especially for negative behaviors | Relatively small number of items in questionnaire to maintain internal reliability may affect results | Study conducted only in Dalian, China; findings may not generalize to other cultural contexts where pedestrian crossing behavior differs
notes: Model 4 (with instrumental attitude, descriptive norm, and conformity tendency) provided the best fit with CFI of 0.996; Sample: 57.3% female, 42.7% male; 49.2% aged 18-24, 33.1% aged 25-39; 93.1% of respondents disapproved of the violating behavior (family mean=2.93, friends mean=3.20 on 7-point scale); High perceived risk of injury (mean=5.77) and danger to life (mean=5.83) did not translate to significant behavioral prediction

title: An extension of the theory of planned behavior to predict pedestrians' violating crossing behavior using structural equation modeling
authors: Hongmei Zhou; Stephanie Ballon Romero; Xiao Qin
research_problem: Examine pedestrians' self-reported violating crossing behavior intentions by applying and extending the Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB), testing instrumental attitude, subjective norm, perceived behavioral control, and extended factors including descriptive norm, perceived risk, and conformity tendency

method_family: Structural Equation Modeling (SEM), Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB) extension
tasks: Predict pedestrians' violating road-crossing behavior intentions
datasets: Survey data from 260 respondents in Dalian, China
metrics: Cronbach's alpha (reliability); Confirmatory Factor Analysis (validity); CMIN/df ratio; RMSEA; PCLOSE; CFI
claim_text: Conformity tendency was a significant predictor in Model 4, indicating that pedestrians are more likely to cross against traffic lights when they observe others doing so
limitations: Self-reported behavior may introduce bias, especially for negative behaviors | Relatively small number of items in questionnaire to maintain internal reliability may affect results | Study conducted only in Dalian, China; findings may not generalize to other cultural contexts where pedestrian crossing behavior differs
notes: Model 4 (with instrumental attitude, descriptive norm, and conformity tendency) provided the best fit with CFI of 0.996; Sample: 57.3% female, 42.7% male; 49.2% aged 18-24, 33.1% aged 25-39; 93.1% of respondents disapproved of the violating behavior (family mean=2.93, friends mean=3.20 on 7-point scale); High perceived risk of injury (mean=5.77) and danger to life (mean=5.83) did not translate to significant behavioral prediction

title: An extension of the theory of planned behavior to predict pedestrians' violating crossing behavior using structural equation modeling
authors: Hongmei Zhou; Stephanie Ballon Romero; Xiao Qin
research_problem: Examine pedestrians' self-reported violating crossing behavior intentions by applying and extending the Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB), testing instrumental attitude, subjective norm, perceived behavioral control, and extended factors including descriptive norm, perceived risk, and conformity tendency
method_summary: Questionnaire-based survey conducted in Dalian, China with 260 respondents. A scenario about crossing against pedestrian traffic lights was presented. Data reliability was assessed using Cronbach's alpha and confirmatory factor analysis. Structural equation modeling (SEM) was used to test four models: basic TPB, TPB with descriptive norm, TPB with perceived risk, and TPB with conformity tendency. Goodness of fit was evaluated using CMIN/df, RMSEA, and CFI indices.
method_family: Structural Equation Modeling (SEM), Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB) extension
tasks: Predict pedestrians' violating road-crossing behavior intentions
datasets: Survey data from 260 respondents in Dalian, China
metrics: Cronbach's alpha (reliability); Confirmatory Factor Analysis (validity); CMIN/df ratio; RMSEA; PCLOSE; CFI
claim_text: Perceived behavioral control was not a significant predictor in any model, likely because crossing the street is not perceived as challenging for able-bodied pedestrians
limitations: Self-reported behavior may introduce bias, especially for negative behaviors | Relatively small number of items in questionnaire to maintain internal reliability may affect results | Study conducted only in Dalian, China; findings may not generalize to other cultural contexts where pedestrian crossing behavior differs
notes: Model 4 (with instrumental attitude, descriptive norm, and conformity tendency) provided the best fit with CFI of 0.996; Sample: 57.3% female, 42.7% male; 49.2% aged 18-24, 33.1% aged 25-39; 93.1% of respondents disapproved of the violating behavior (family mean=2.93, friends mean=3.20 on 7-point scale); High perceived risk of injury (mean=5.77) and danger to life (mean=5.83) did not translate to significant behavioral prediction

title: An extension of the theory of planned behavior to predict pedestrians' violating crossing behavior using structural equation modeling
authors: Hongmei Zhou; Stephanie Ballon Romero; Xiao Qin
research_problem: Examine pedestrians' self-reported violating crossing behavior intentions by applying and extending the Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB), testing instrumental attitude, subjective norm, perceived behavioral control, and extended factors including descriptive norm, perceived risk, and conformity tendency

Goodness of fit was evaluated using CMIN/df, RMSEA, and CFI indices.
method_family: Structural Equation Modeling (SEM), Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB) extension
tasks: Predict pedestrians' violating road-crossing behavior intentions
datasets: Survey data from 260 respondents in Dalian, China
metrics: Cronbach's alpha (reliability); Confirmatory Factor Analysis (validity); CMIN/df ratio; RMSEA; PCLOSE; CFI
claim_text: Perceived risk was not a significant predictor, suggesting pedestrians fail to adequately perceive the susceptibility and seriousness of risks associated with traffic violations
limitations: Self-reported behavior may introduce bias, especially for negative behaviors | Relatively small number of items in questionnaire to maintain internal reliability may affect results | Study conducted only in Dalian, China; findings may not generalize to other cultural contexts where pedestrian crossing behavior differs
notes: Model 4 (with instrumental attitude, descriptive norm, and conformity tendency) provided the best fit with CFI of 0.996; Sample: 57.3% female, 42.7% male; 49.2% aged 18-24, 33.1% aged 25-39; 93.1% of respondents disapproved of the violating behavior (family mean=2.93, friends mean=3.20 on 7-point scale); High perceived risk of injury (mean=5.77) and danger to life (mean=5.83) did not translate to significant behavioral prediction
